title: Layout of the decision detail
question: When you open a decision, how should its detail be laid out so it's easy to recall and to check before adding a feature, without a wall of text?
rationale: Calm by default in a clear top-down order — phase chip, title, the question as a framing deck, then the chosen option in a light answer card and the 'why' as its own readable block, then impact and the foldable comparison. Chosen to fix the recurring pain of too much text.

history.from: Calm by default, reveal on tap (choice, why, and impact first)
history.reason: Same calm, choice-first structure, but the panel was rebuilt for scannability and an Apple-standard look: the question moved to a framing deck above the answer (it had been folded into the why), the rationale was lifted into its own readable prose block out of the tinted box, the chosen option became a light answer card, and section labels gained real type hierarchy. A layout and readability change, not a re-organization.

options.label: Calm by default, reveal on tap (choice, why, and impact first)

tradeoffs.note: shows choice, why, and impact; the rest is one tap away
'Affects' up top shows what a change would touch at a glance
the full comparison is a tap away, not instant

options.label: Show everything at once (leave as is)

tradeoffs.note: every option and pro/con shown at once = wall of text

options.label: Put the full comparison front and center

tradeoffs.note: leads with the densest block every time
good for re-arguing a choice, worse for quick recall
